section.title: Solomon's Prayer of Dedication
section.sectionRationale: Standing on a bronze platform before the whole assembly, Solomon blesses the LORD, recounts the promise made to David, and then kneels with hands spread toward heaven to pray the great dedicatory prayer. He confesses that no house, not even the highest heavens, can contain God, then pleads that the LORD would nevertheless hear every prayer prayed toward this place, whether from a repentant Israelite, a foreigner drawn by God's name, or an exile in a distant land.
section.thesis: Solomon's prayer holds together the whole tension of the temple: God is far too great to be contained by any house, yet He graciously binds Himself to hear the prayers of His people, and even outsiders, who call on Him toward this place in humility and repentance.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Solomon blesses the LORD and recounts the promise to David, verses 1 to 11; the prayer opens by confessing the God who cannot be contained yet hears prayer, verses 12 to 21; seven petitions covering sin, defeat, drought, famine, the foreigner, and war, verses 22 to 39; and the closing plea for mercy and the request that the LORD arise and enter His resting place, verses 40 to 42.

units.label: Solomon blesses the LORD and recounts the promise
units.note: what He spoke, He has fulfilled

verses.text: Then Solomon declared: "The LORD has said that He would dwell in the thick cloud.
But I have built You an exalted house, a place for You to dwell forever."
And as the whole assembly of Israel stood there, the king turned around and blessed them all
and said: "Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, who has fulfilled with His own hand what He spoke with His mouth to my father David, saying,

'Since the day I brought My people out of the land of Egypt, I have not chosen a city from any tribe of Israel in which to build a house so that My {{shem|Name}} would be there, nor have I chosen anyone to be ruler over My people Israel.
But now I have chosen Jerusalem for My Name to be there, and I have chosen David to be over My people Israel.'
Now it was in the heart of my father David to build a house for the Name of the LORD, the God of Israel.
But the LORD said to my father David, 'Since it was in your heart to build a house for My Name, you have done well to have this in your heart.
Nevertheless, you are not the one to build it, but your son, your own offspring, will build the house for My Name.'

Now the LORD has fulfilled the word that He spoke. I have succeeded my father David, and I sit on the throne of Israel, as the LORD promised. I have built the house for the Name of the LORD, the God of Israel.
And there I have placed the ark, which contains the {{berit|covenant}} of the LORD that He made with the children of Israel."

units.label: The prayer of dedication begins
units.note: the God who cannot be contained yet hears

verses.text: Then Solomon stood before the altar of the LORD in front of the whole assembly of Israel and spread out his hands.

Now Solomon had made a bronze platform five cubits long, five cubits wide, and three cubits high and had placed it in the middle of the courtyard. He stood on it, knelt down before the whole assembly of Israel, spread out his hands toward {{shamayim|heaven}},
and said: "O LORD, God of Israel, there is no God like You in heaven or on earth, keeping Your covenant of {{chesed|loving devotion}} with Your servants who walk before You with all their hearts.
You have kept Your promise to Your servant, my father David. What You spoke with Your mouth You have fulfilled with Your hand this day.

Therefore now, O LORD, God of Israel, keep for Your servant, my father David, what You promised when You said: 'You will never fail to have a man to sit before Me on the throne of Israel, if only your descendants guard their way to walk in My law as you have walked before Me.'
And now, O LORD, God of Israel, please confirm what You promised to Your servant David.
But will God indeed dwell with man upon the earth? The heavens, even the highest heavens, cannot contain You, much less this temple I have built.
Yet regard the {{tefillah|prayer}} and plea of Your servant, O LORD my God, so that You may hear the cry and the prayer that Your servant is praying before You.
May Your eyes be open toward this temple day and night, toward the place where You said You would put Your Name, so that You may hear the prayer that Your servant prays toward this place.
Hear the plea of Your servant and of Your people Israel when they pray toward this place. May You hear from heaven, Your dwelling place. May You hear and forgive.

units.label: Seven petitions for Israel and the nations
units.note: sin, defeat, drought, famine, the foreigner, war

verses.text: When a man sins against his neighbor and is required to take an oath, and he comes to take an oath before Your altar in this temple,

then may You hear from heaven and act. May You judge Your servants, condemning the wicked man by bringing down on his own head what he has done, and justifying the righteous man by rewarding him according to his righteousness.
When Your people Israel are defeated before an enemy because they have sinned against You, and they return to You and confess Your name, praying and pleading before You in this temple,
then may You hear from heaven and forgive the sin of Your people Israel. May You restore them to the land You gave to them and their fathers.

When the skies are shut and there is no rain because Your people have sinned against You, and they pray toward this place and confess Your name, and they turn from their sins because You have afflicted them,

then may You hear from heaven and forgive the sin of Your servants, Your people Israel, so that You may teach them the good way in which they should walk. May You send rain on the land that You gave Your people as an inheritance.
When famine or plague comes upon the land, or blight or mildew or locusts or grasshoppers, or when their enemies besiege them in their cities, whatever plague or sickness may come,
then may whatever prayer or plea Your people Israel make, each knowing his own afflictions and spreading out his hands toward this temple,
be heard by You from heaven, Your dwelling place. And may You forgive and repay each man according to all his ways, since You know his heart, for You alone know the hearts of men,
so that they may fear You and walk in Your ways all the days they live in the land that You gave to our fathers.

And as for the foreigner who is not of Your people Israel but has come from a distant land because of Your great name and Your mighty hand and outstretched arm, when he comes and prays toward this temple,

then may You hear from heaven, Your dwelling place, and do according to all for which the foreigner calls to You. Then all the peoples of the earth will know Your name and fear You, as do Your people Israel, and they will know that this house I have built is called by Your Name.
When Your people go to war against their enemies, wherever You send them, and when they pray to You in the direction of this city You have chosen and the house I have built for Your Name,
then may You hear from heaven their prayer and their plea, and may You uphold their cause.
When they sin against You, for there is no one who does not sin, and You become angry with them and deliver them to an enemy who takes them as captives to a land far or near,

and when they come to their senses in the land to which they were taken, and they repent and plead with You in the land of their captors, saying, 'We have sinned and done wrong; we have acted wickedly,'

and when they return to You with all their heart and soul in the land of the enemies who took them captive, and when they pray in the direction of the land that You gave to their fathers, the city You have chosen, and the house I have built for Your Name,
then may You hear from heaven, Your dwelling place, their prayer and pleas, and may You uphold their cause. May You forgive Your people who sinned against You.

units.label: The closing plea and the LORD's anointed
units.note: arise, O LORD God, and enter Your resting place

verses.text: Now, my God, may Your eyes be open and Your ears attentive to the prayer offered in this place.
Now therefore, arise, O LORD God, and enter Your resting place, You and the ark of Your might. May Your priests, O LORD God, be clothed with salvation, and may Your godly ones rejoice in goodness.
O LORD God, do not reject Your {{mashiach|anointed one}}. Remember Your loving devotion to Your servant David."

shem.gloss: Name. Repeated throughout the prayer as the shem of the LORD that dwells at the temple, verses 5, 6, 7, 9, 10, 20, 33, 34, 38; God's Name represents His revealed character and presence, so that prayer offered toward this place is prayer offered toward God Himself, not toward a mere building.

berit.gloss: Covenant. The ark placed in the temple contains the berit of the LORD made with Israel at Horeb, and the whole prayer that follows appeals again and again to God's covenant loyalty, His chesed, toward David and his descendants.

shamayim.gloss: Heaven, heavens. Solomon spreads his hands toward shamayim, verse 13, then confesses that even the shamayim, the highest heavens, cannot contain God, verse 18, before repeatedly asking the LORD to hear from shamayim, His true dwelling place, verses 21, 23, 25, 27, 30, 33, 35, 39.

chesed.gloss: Loving devotion, covenant faithfulness. Solomon grounds his whole prayer in the LORD's chesed toward David, verse 14 and verse 42, the steadfast love that guarantees the promise of an enduring throne will hold even when Israel's own faithfulness fails.

tefillah.gloss: Prayer, plea, intercession. Solomon's entire address to God is his tefillah, verse 19, and the word threads through the whole chapter as the substance of what the LORD is asked to hear, whether prayed by Israelite, foreigner, or exile.

mashiach.gloss: Anointed one, the king set apart by anointing oil to rule under God. Solomon's closing plea, do not reject Your anointed one, invokes the Davidic king as God's chosen representative, language that ultimately points to the Messiah, the great Anointed One in whom God's covenant loyalty to David finds its everlasting fulfillment.

basic.context: Solomon blesses the LORD before the whole assembly, recalling how God promised David that his son would build the temple and has now fulfilled that word. Then Solomon kneels on a bronze platform, spreads his hands toward heaven, and prays the great dedicatory prayer. He confesses that not even the highest heavens can contain God, yet asks Him to hear every prayer prayed toward this place, whether an oath about a dispute, national defeat, drought, famine, the prayer of a believing foreigner, war, or even the repentance of Israel exiled in a distant land. He closes by asking the LORD to arise and enter His resting place and not reject His anointed king.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a specific, historic prayer offered at a unique dedication; it is not a liturgical script to repeat verbatim today. What is timelessly true and directly applicable is the prayer's theology: God cannot be contained by any structure, yet He graciously binds Himself to hear the prayers of His genuinely repentant people, and even outsiders, who call on Him in faith.
basic.originalLanguages: Solomon appeals throughout to God's berit, His covenant, and chesed, His loving devotion, toward David, while confessing that the shamayim, the heavens, cannot contain the God whose shem, Name, he asks would dwell at the temple. His entire address is called his tefillah, prayer, a word repeated to unite every petition, and it closes with a plea on behalf of God's mashiach, His anointed king.
basic.typology: The prayer's confession that heaven cannot contain God, yet God graciously dwells among His people and hears their prayer, anticipates Christ, in whom the fullness of deity dwells bodily while remaining the infinite God. The plea, do not reject Your anointed one, reaches beyond Solomon and even David's line as it stood then, toward the Messiah, the ultimate Anointed One whom the Father never rejects but always hears.
basic.christ: Solomon's confession, will God indeed dwell with man upon the earth, is answered fully in Jesus, in whom God dwelt among us in a way no temple could achieve, John 1:14, and who called His own body the true temple, greater than Solomon's, John 2:19-21. The prayer's welcome of the foreigner who prays toward this temple, verses 32-33, previews the gospel's welcome of the nations through Christ, and the closing plea for God's anointed finds its fullest answer in Jesus, God's Anointed One, whom the Father never rejects but always hears, Hebrews 7:25.
basic.god: God is transcendent beyond measure, so vast that the highest heavens cannot contain Him, yet He graciously chooses to place His Name at the temple and hear every prayer directed toward it. He is unwaveringly covenant keeping, having fulfilled with His hand what He spoke with His mouth to David, and He is a God of astonishing forgiveness, ready to hear and restore His people through sin, defeat, drought, famine, and even exile, provided they turn back to Him wholeheartedly.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Kings 8 gives the parallel account of this same prayer. Exodus 40:34-38 describes the glory cloud filling the tabernacle, the pattern this dedication echoes. Acts 7:48-50 echoes Solomon's own confession that the Most High does not dwell in houses made by human hands. And John 1:14 declares that the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, the ultimate filling of God's presence in a human life.
basic.oneStory: Solomon's prayer holds together the whole story's central tension: God is too great for any house, yet He graciously draws near to hear His people, and even outsiders, when they call on Him. That tension finds its full resolution in Jesus, the Word who tabernacled among us, the true temple greater than Solomon's, and the Anointed One whom the Father never rejects but always hears.

context.literary: The chapter unfolds in four movements: a blessing that recalls the promise to David, verses 1 to 11; the prayer's opening confession of God's transcendence and nearness, verses 12 to 21; a series of seven repeating petitions, when... then may You hear from heaven, applied to a growing range of scenarios from individual disputes to national exile, verses 22 to 39; and a closing appeal for the LORD to arise and for His anointed to be remembered, verses 40 to 42, which the Chronicler alone includes, drawing on Psalm 132.
context.historical: The bronze platform Solomon stood on, five cubits square and three cubits high, allowed the whole assembly to see the king kneeling in prayer rather than merely standing, a detail unique to Chronicles that underscores the humility of even Israel's greatest king before God. The prayer's petitions, covering drought, famine, siege, and exile, read almost as a preview of the very judgments that would later fall on Judah, judgments the Chronicler's first exilic readers had already lived through.
context.canonical: This prayer parallels 1 Kings 8:22-53 but the Chronicler's distinct closing, verses 40 to 42, draws directly on Psalm 132:8-10, replacing Kings' final appeal to the exodus with an appeal to God's covenant loyalty to David and His anointed, deliberately steering the returned exiles' hope toward the promised line that would one day yield the Messiah.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To preserve Solomon's prayer as the theological charter for how Israel, and even the nations, were to relate to the temple, praying toward it in confident trust that the transcendent God who cannot be contained would nevertheless hear from heaven and act, and to root the exiled readers' hope in the covenant loyalty owed to David and his anointed line.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter records a specific, historic prayer offered at a unique dedication; it does not prescribe that believers must pray facing a particular building today. What is prescriptive and enduring is the prayer's theology of God's transcendence and nearness, His covenant faithfulness, and His readiness to forgive a genuinely repentant people, truths that outlast the temple itself and are fulfilled in Christ.

debates.question: How can Solomon say God will dwell with man on the earth, verse 18, while also confessing the heavens cannot contain Him?

views.label: A real, localized presence without confinement
views.case: The glory cloud of chapter 5 demonstrates that the LORD truly manifests His presence at the temple in a special way, choosing it as the place where His Name dwells and prayer is directed, without that localized presence limiting or exhausting His infinite being, which fills heaven and earth, Jeremiah 23:24.

views.label: A rhetorical safeguard against pagan temple theology
views.case: Solomon's careful qualification distinguishes Israel's temple from the pagan notion that a god is bound to or housed within a shrine; the temple is not a container for God but a chosen meeting place for prayer, a distinction the refrain, hear from heaven, consistently reinforces.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the temple never contains God but is the appointed place toward which prayer is directed and from which God graciously answers, a truth carried forward into the New Testament's teaching that believers themselves are now the temple of the Holy Spirit, 1 Corinthians 6:19.

debates.question: Why does the Chronicler's version of the prayer end differently from the version in 1 Kings 8?

views.label: A deliberate shift toward messianic hope
views.case: By closing with an appeal to Psalm 132 and the LORD's anointed rather than Kings' appeal to the exodus, the Chronicler, writing after the exile with no reigning king, steers his readers' hope toward the promised Davidic line and its ultimate fulfillment rather than backward to Egypt.

views.label: A liturgical adaptation for temple use
views.case: Some scholars suggest the Chronicler draws on language already used in temple worship, such as the procession psalm behind Psalm 132, to connect Solomon's prayer with the ongoing liturgical life of Israel's worship, regardless of political circumstance.

debates.bearing: Either way, the effect for the Chronicler's exilic readers is the same: hope is anchored not in a building they had lost but in God's covenant loyalty to His anointed, a hope that finds its answer in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The prayer's vocabulary reinforces its central tension: the berit, covenant, made with Israel rests in the ark, and Solomon repeatedly appeals to God's chesed, loving devotion, toward David, even as he confesses that the shamayim, the heavens, cannot contain the God whose shem, Name, he asks would dwell there. His entire address is called his tefillah, prayer, and it closes with a plea that God would not reject His mashiach, His anointed king, language that reaches toward the Messiah in whom God's covenant loyalty to David is fulfilled forever.

typology.type: The temple as the place where God's Name dwells and prayer is heard, though heaven cannot contain Him
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the greater temple, in whom the fullness of deity dwells bodily while remaining the infinite God, Colossians 2:9, John 1:14.

typology.type: Solomon's plea, do not reject Your anointed one
typology.fulfillment: Christ, God's ultimate Anointed One, whom the Father never rejects but always hears on behalf of His people, Hebrews 7:25.

typology.type: The prayer's welcome of the foreigner who prays toward the house
typology.fulfillment: The gospel's welcome of the nations into God's household through Christ, Ephesians 2:13-19.

study.christ: Solomon's confession, will God indeed dwell with man upon the earth, finds its answer in the incarnation, when the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, John 1:14, and in Jesus' own claim to be a temple greater than this one, able to be destroyed and raised in three days, John 2:19-21. The prayer's welcome of the foreigner who comes because of God's Name, verses 32-33, previews the gospel breaking down every wall of hostility to welcome the nations into God's family in Christ, Ephesians 2:13-19, and its closing plea, do not reject Your anointed one, reaches at last to Jesus, God's Anointed One, whom the Father never rejects but always hears as He continually intercedes for His people, Hebrews 7:25.
study.god: God is transcendent beyond measure, so that the highest heavens cannot contain Him, yet He graciously chooses to place His Name at the temple and to hear the prayers of His people directed toward it. He is unwaveringly covenant keeping, having fulfilled with His hand what He spoke with His mouth to David, and He is a God of astonishing forgiveness, ready to hear and restore His people through sin, military defeat, drought, famine, and even exile in a foreign land, provided they turn back to Him with a whole heart. He is also a God who welcomes outsiders and never finally rejects His anointed one.

crossReferences.note: The parallel account of Solomon's dedicatory prayer.
The source of the Chronicler's distinct closing plea, arise, O LORD, to Your resting place, and do not reject Your anointed one.
The Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, and we have seen His glory, the ultimate filling of God's presence in a human life.
Christ always lives to intercede for those who come to God through Him, the fulfillment of the plea that God's anointed never be rejected.

study.oneStory: Solomon's prayer holds together the whole story's tension: God is too great for any house, yet He graciously draws near to hear His people, and even outsiders, when they call on Him. Its closing plea for God's anointed reaches beyond Solomon's own throne to the Messiah in whom that tension is finally resolved, the Word who tabernacled among us, the true temple greater than Solomon's, in whom the transcendent God has drawn permanently near.

sermon.title: God's Dwelling Place
sermon.why: Preached December 9, 2018 on 2 Chronicles 6:18, this message from Truth For Life works directly from Solomon's confession that heaven cannot contain God, showing how the incarnation answers that very tension, an exact match for this study's central theme.

quiz.q: What did Solomon confess about God even as he dedicated the temple to Him, verse 18?

options.text: That the heavens, even the highest heavens, cannot contain God, much less the temple he had built.
options.note: Verse 18 states this confession directly.

options.text: That the temple now fully contained God's presence with nothing left outside it.
options.note: Solomon explicitly denies this is possible; the temple cannot contain God.

options.text: That God had abandoned Israel and no longer heard prayer.
options.note: The prayer's entire thrust is the opposite, confidence that God does hear prayer from heaven.

options.text: The prayer contains no reflection on God's greatness at all.
options.note: Verses 14 and 18 both contain extended reflection on God's incomparable greatness.

quiz.q: What request does Solomon make on behalf of the foreigner who is not of Israel, verses 32-33?

options.text: That God would hear and answer the foreigner's prayer toward the temple, so that all peoples of the earth would know and fear God's Name.
options.note: Verses 32-33 make this explicit petition for outsiders drawn by God's reputation.

options.text: That foreigners should be barred entirely from praying toward the temple.
options.note: The opposite is true; Solomon specifically asks that foreigners be heard.

options.text: That foreigners must first become Israelite citizens before any prayer is heard.
options.note: The text sets no such requirement; it simply asks God to answer the foreigner who prays because of His Name.

options.text: The prayer contains no mention of foreigners at all.
options.note: Verses 32-33 explicitly address the foreigner's prayer.

quiz.q: What does Solomon pray regarding Israel's possible future exile, verses 36-39?

options.text: That if the exiled people repent with all their heart and pray toward the land, city, and temple, God would hear from heaven, forgive their sins, and uphold their cause.
options.note: Verses 36-39 lay out this exact scenario and plea for restoration after judgment.

options.text: That exile would never happen to Israel under any circumstances.
options.note: The prayer assumes exile is a real possibility, for there is no one who does not sin, verse 36, rather than denying it could occur.

options.text: That exiled Israelites should be permanently abandoned with no hope of restoration.
options.note: The prayer's entire thrust is hope for forgiveness and restoration upon repentance, not abandonment.

options.text: The prayer says nothing about exile or captivity.
options.note: Verses 36-39 directly address the scenario of exile and captivity.

quiz.q: How does Solomon's prayer close, and what is distinctive about that ending in Chronicles?

options.text: With a plea for the LORD to arise and enter His resting place and not reject His anointed one, language the Chronicler draws from Psalm 132.
options.note: Verses 40-42 give this closing plea, which differs from the ending recorded in 1 Kings 8 and echoes Psalm 132:8-10.

options.text: With a curse against Israel's enemies.
options.note: The closing is a plea for God's presence and mercy toward His anointed and His people, not a curse against enemies.

options.text: With silence; the prayer simply stops without any closing words.
options.note: Verses 40-42 record clear, deliberate closing words.

options.text: The prayer has no distinct ending in Chronicles compared to Kings.
options.note: The Chronicler's ending is notably distinct, drawing on Psalm 132 rather than repeating the ending found in 1 Kings 8.

quiz.q: What is the underlying theological tension Solomon holds together throughout the prayer?

options.text: That God is too vast for any house to contain, yet He graciously chooses to hear prayer directed toward the place where His Name dwells.
options.note: This tension runs through verses 18-21 and the entire structure of the prayer's petitions.

options.text: That God can be fully controlled by human ritual once a temple is built.
options.note: The prayer explicitly denies any such control; it asks God to graciously hear, not claims to compel Him.

options.text: That prayer is pointless since God does not listen to requests.
options.note: The prayer's repeated refrain, may You hear from heaven, assumes and pleads for God's attentive listening.

options.text: There is no tension in the prayer at all; it is a simple, one dimensional request.
options.note: The prayer explicitly wrestles with the tension between God's transcendence and His willingness to hear, verses 18-21.

questions: Solomon confessed that even the highest heavens cannot contain God, yet asked Him to hear prayer from heaven. How does holding together God's vastness and His nearness shape the way you pray?
Solomon prayed for the foreigner who comes from a distant land because of God's Name. Who in your life is an outsider you could be praying will come to know and fear the LORD?

The prayer anticipates a day when Israel might sin, suffer, and need to repent and return with all their heart. Is there an area where you need to return to God today rather than waiting for a crisis to force you back?
Solomon closed by pleading that God would not reject His anointed one. How does knowing that Christ, God's true Anointed, is never rejected by the Father give you confidence in your own prayers?
This prayer covers sin, defeat, drought, and exile, real hardships of ordinary life. What hardship are you facing this week that you could bring honestly before God in prayer?

translationNotes.note: The bronze platform Solomon stood and knelt on is unique to the Chronicler's account and is not mentioned in the parallel passage in 1 Kings 8, adding the detail that the king knelt rather than simply stood to pray.

For there is no one who does not sin reflects a realistic biblical anthropology found throughout Scripture, for example 1 Kings 8:46 and Romans 3:23, underlying the prayer's expectation that exile due to sin is a real possibility.

This closing plea is distinct to the Chronicler and draws directly on Psalm 132:8-10, replacing the ending found in 1 Kings 8:53-61 with language rooted in God's covenant loyalty to David and his anointed line.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Chronicles 6:1-42. No em dashes or en dashes remain; all such marks in the source were converted to commas.
